"The stablecoin requirements under Genius conflict with the requirements under MiCA, which is candidly nonsensical considering the whole point of crypto and stablecoins"

Katherine on the contradiction between Genius and MiCA that's frustrating global crypto companies

"The big difference here is they want to cover tokenized assets more generally, but they also want to cover stablecoins issued by non-EU stablecoin issuers. One source of frustration for anyone that works at a global crypto company is that the stablecoin requirements under Genius conflict with the requirements under MiCA"

"You have a slew of stablecoin issuers with either a single issuance model, I believe Circle has single issuance, versus a multi-issuance model like MoonPay, which has multiple entities in different jurisdictions issuing different stables. What MiCA is now saying is, sorry US and non-EU stablecoin issuers, you have to get in line, you're going to have comparable standards to the EU issuers"

"Everyone has a few months, till September I think, to provide input. So we won't see proposed regulatory revisions for at least a year probably. But what's happening in my mind is that Europe is kind of freaking out in response to the growth of tokenization and stables, and they're figuring out how to fix this"

tweets.text:
I think the labs are basically done on AI prose.

I don't think they'll get that much better from here. None of the labs are losing customers because of prose. They are already better than 99% of humans, and for the top 1%, they know how to edit AI prose. The models have genuinely landed in a basin of "very good writers" and yes, the models have tics, but I think we actually prefer that at a societal level.

Those tics may change after a while across model generations, like when Target changes its seasonal clothing line to not look like last year. It moves around a bit to not look cheap. But at a societal level, we will adapt and be able to identify mass produced clothes.
tweets.quoted_tweet_text: AI writing is so good now, there are only a handful of idiosyncrasies left to point out. Those will vanish shortly.

Frontier AIs are genuinely very good writers.

thread_tweets.text:
Yes but the question is why they don't want to read AI written stuff.

Think of yourself: when you ask Claude to explain something to you, do you read its explanation and say "wow this is awful writing" or do you come away with "wow, that was an excellent explanation, way better than I'd get asking someone else" Because that's what I come away with 99% of the time.

The reason why we recoil at AI writing is rarely because the writing is actually bad. 

It's because it's lazy.

Because reading someone's writing is meant to create an empathetic window into a writer's mind, and the writer who is just proxying AI is implicitly saying "you are not worth my time."

tweets.text:
The Fed is not hiking. This print just started a new disinflationary trend that will last at least a few prints and provide them enough to lean on to stay on hold.

As Warsh repeats every time he speaks, it's the balance sheet that needs reform. The yield curve is historically flat and creates imbalanced policy transmission between small and large businesses, main street and wall street. This is what needs tightening/restricting, not the front end rate. A smaller Fed footprint in Treasury duration fixes this.

tweets.text:
That was my exact point. Communication isn't inherently bad. Waller changing his reaction function months after the peak in core inflation is an incorrect interpretation of the data and a mistake. It is equivalent to buying 2yr notes at 3.4% on Feb 27th and selling them yesterday at 4.25%. It's also not just him though. The recent FOMC minutes showed the committee dramatically increased their inflation outlook from their April to June meeting despite the price of oil decreasing by -30%. If they are worried about clear communication and credibility, they should look in the mirror.

I don't think Warsh is against communication but if the quality of communication is consistently poor, it needs to be addressed/discontinued. The problems likely are related to everyone consciously or subconsciously vying for relevance and attention in their speeches/comments by attempting to say something novel, right or wrong.

yup... its almost certainly productivity enhancing, the question is how much (and vs. what's priced in for markets).  Macro data is one lens and suggests whatever it is, it's not meaningfully moving the needle vs. other things.

That's the challenge, lots of things are productivity enhancing, but for it to *accelerate* productivity it has to be better than the inventions of the past which is a high bar.

tweets.text:
Two different capacity expansion announcements today across $TSEM and $AAOI.

Tower Semi, supported by the Japanese government, announced expansion of its 300mm Silicon Photonics (SiPho), Silicon Germanium (SiGe), and advanced packaging capabilities. 

Targeting $1.2B net profit, and $3.6B revenue in 2028 (with a ~$28B MC, that's ~23.3 2028 forward p/e).

AAOI announced the commencement of its buildout, roughly 400,000 square feet of manufacturing to make 800g/1.6T production go brrr. 

The overall expansion is also supported by the local city government in Texas. (they also $20.85M worth of subsidies from the State earlier).

This one isn't exactly as materially new as Tower, since AAOI originally announced these plans before. Just commencement of it. 

Regardless, glad to see both of these companies in their Anime training arc phase as they power up.

tweets.text:
The Tech Tree of Civilization has been achieved to these dominant driving factors:

> The first being humans mastering their environment through language and technology. All of which allows less cognitive work load to be spent on survival and instead cognitive capacity on technology. Humans need to spend less time on food, shelter and the environment and could spend more time on improving their environment which was a key characteristic of the agricultural revolution. 

> Infrastructure and automation that further removed humans from any of the systems they have built when it comes to manual labour. This was a key characteristic of the Industrial revolution where humans spent less time doing manual labour and shifted most of their efforts towards cognitive tasks which accelerated technological development.

> A large part of unlocking the tech tree was driven by war and facing adversaries. During the Great War of the 20th century this resulted in many era defining scientific breakthroughs such as atomic technology and the mass mobilization of the workforce showing factories/automation are the key to industrial power and supremacy.

> Now we stand at the precipice of automating cognitive labour in which systems that may exceed our own intelligence will be able to unlock future parts of the tech tree without the need for cognitive labour. Technology will proceed to advancement without at large the intervention of humans if we achieve AGI/ASI.

> Robotics is the final piece of the puzzle when it comes to any remaining human labour and the automation of our Industrial processes for anything a consumer will need.

The defining arc of human civilisation has been reducing the need for cognitive load for survival to be spent time on unlocking parts of the tech tree to improve our quality of life on Earth.

The question will become what will humans do with their time if this cognitive labour which we have so far leaned on is at large automated by systems more powerful than our cognitive aparatus. How can we live meaningful lives without falling into the leasure traps from the Information Age Industrial boom. 

My hope at large is the following. For the majority of history we have looked outward to ensure our own survival and we have rejected at large our inner world which holds knowledge and wisdom I believe collectively is untapped.

To understand what our place is within the universe, our own sentience, what meaning lies outside of labour and the deepest questions at it relates to our universe through physics and mathematics leading to a new enlightenment era. 

This would be my hope over the centuries to come.

thread_tweets.text: It's interesting in this case how well AI fits into existing trends. Programming wasn't evolving in this direction because AI was coming. No one knew it was. And yet we end up with what looks like a smooth acceleration along much the same path.

thread_tweets.text: This often happens with technology. Probably more often than not. It's as if the system as a whole has some kind of foresight that individual participants in it don't have.

tweets.full_text:
Before vibe coding became a thing, programming was already evolving in that direction. It already increasingly consisted of installing and configuring stuff other people wrote, without reading the source.

It's interesting in this case how well AI fits into existing trends. Programming wasn't evolving in this direction because AI was coming. No one knew it was. And yet we end up with what looks like a smooth acceleration along much the same path.

This often happens with technology. Probably more often than not. It's as if the system as a whole has some kind of foresight that individual participants in it don't have.

#Uranium #Nuclear #EnergySecurity

Every new nuclear build raises one increasingly important question:

Where will the uranium come from?

India's largest power producer, NTPC, is now looking beyond long-term supply contracts. It's actively seeking investments in uranium projects across Canada, Australia, Kazakhstan and South Africa to help fuel the 30 GW of nuclear capacity it plans to build over the next two decades.

That's a powerful signal.

As countries race to secure future fuel supplies, large, district-scale uranium projects in stable jurisdictions become increasingly strategic assets.

That's one reason I'm watching $M.CN closely.

Myriad Uranium is currently drilling exciting new targets at Copper Mountain, Wyoming, outside the estimated 655 million pounds of historical endowment. This could become the largest uranium project in the United States imo.

If utilities, governments and strategic investors begin competing not just for pounds—but for ownership of future production—the value of quality exploration projects could look very different than it does today.

Timing matters. So does scale.

Disclosure: Paid partnership with Myriad Uranium. Not financial advice. Always do your own due diligence.

We didn't just beat consensus, we beat our own forecast

Yesterday, our Nowcast IQ models held the SOFTEST US inflation forecast of anyone on the street. This morning, the BLS proved us right — and then some.

The scoreboard:

Consensus: -0.1% MoM headline, +0.2% core
Nowcast IQ: -0.27% headline, +0.13-0.14% core
Actual: -0.4% headline, 0.0% core

We were ~20bps below the entire street. Reality came in below even us — in exactly the direction we called, on exactly the components we flagged (gasoline demolition, tame core goods, medical care deflating, no food pass-through).

Why does this matter? Because Waller put a July HIKE on the table 24 hours before the print. Williams drew the line at 0.2% core. Our models said the print would come in UNDER that threshold with high conviction — and told our subscribers to position for the hike getting priced OUT, not in. That is exactly what is happening as I write.

This wasn't luck. It's the same machine that has kept our subscribers on the right side of the inflation cycle all year — built on high-frequency price data, not vibes, not consensus-hugging.

And the part I enjoy the most: this beat was delivered from a sunbed in Corsica. Wall Street has the PhDs, the terminals and the midtown offices. We have better models and a pool.

If you want the forecast BEFORE the print instead of the explanation after it, you know where to find us. Nowcast IQ is live for all Steno Research subscribers.

#macro #inflation #CPI #FederalReserve #nowcasting

$ZIM : 𝗢𝗻𝗲 𝗼𝗳 𝘁𝗵𝗲 𝗠𝗼𝘀𝘁 𝗠𝗶𝘀𝗽𝗿𝗶𝗰𝗲𝗱 𝗖𝗼𝗺𝗽𝗮𝗻𝗶𝗲𝘀 𝗶𝗻 𝘁𝗵𝗲 𝗠𝗮𝗿𝗸𝗲𝘁

𝗠𝗮𝗿𝗸𝗲𝘁 𝗖𝗮𝗽: ~$3.0B
𝗖𝗮𝘀𝗵 𝗼𝗻 𝗛𝗮𝗻𝗱: ~$2.6B
𝗖𝗮𝘀𝗵 𝗳𝗿𝗼𝗺 𝗢𝗽𝗲𝗿𝗮𝘁𝗶𝗼𝗻𝘀 𝘁𝗼 𝗗𝗮𝘁𝗲: ~$600M
𝗘𝗻𝘁𝗲𝗿𝗽𝗿𝗶𝘀𝗲 𝗩𝗮𝗹𝘂𝗲: -$200M (𝗡𝗲𝗴𝗮𝘁𝗶𝘃𝗲)

With the $ZIM buyout now practically dead due to government opposition, a great opportunity has emerged from the ashes.

The market is effectively valuing one of the world’s youngest and most efficient container fleets, a leading Trans-Pacific carrier, and billions of dollars of earnings potential at a negative $200 million enterprise value. Insane.

$HLAG and $MAERSK recently updated their outlooks, the positive news sent both stocks up around 7%. We believe $ZIM should benefit even more, as it has one of the highest sensitivities to spot freight rates. We expect the company to report strong results soon.

𝗙𝗿𝗲𝗶𝗴𝗵𝘁 𝗥𝗮𝘁𝗲𝘀

Based on current freight rates, we estimate $ZIM is currently earning approximately $0.7 to $0.8 per share every week.

Peak season is only beginning. Traditionally it starts in July and August ahead of the Christmas shopping season. This year, however, demand strengthened unusually early, with peak conditions beginning as early as May due to a shortage of available vessels.

𝗪𝗵𝘆 𝘁𝗵𝗲 𝗦𝗵𝗶𝗽𝗽𝗶𝗻𝗴 𝗠𝗮𝗿𝗸𝗲𝘁 𝗥𝗲𝗺𝗮𝗶𝗻𝘀 𝗧𝗶𝗴𝗵𝘁

For years analysts have predicted oversupply. They continue to ignore that effective capacity, not theoretical capacity, determines freight rates.

𝟭. 𝗦𝘂𝗲𝘇 𝗖𝗮𝗻𝗮𝗹 𝗗𝗶𝘀𝗿𝘂𝗽𝘁𝗶𝗼𝗻

The Red Sea situation continues forcing many vessels around the Cape of Good Hope, extending voyage times and effectively removing an estimated 8-10% of global container shipping capacity. Longer voyages mean fewer vessels are available for new cargo until normal Suez traffic resumes.

𝟮. 𝗣𝗼𝗿𝘁 𝗖𝗼𝗻𝗴𝗲𝘀𝘁𝗶𝗼𝗻

Ports around the world remain congested while very few major new ports are being built. Every additional day ships spend waiting effectively removes capacity from the global fleet, further tightening vessel availability.

𝟯. 𝗦𝘁𝗿𝗼𝗻𝗴 𝗚𝗹𝗼𝗯𝗮𝗹 𝗧𝗿𝗮𝗱𝗲

Global trade continues growing faster than expected.

2024: ~7%
2025: ~4.8%
2026: We expect ~5%

Demand continues surprising to the upside.

The result is simple.

Analysts correctly predicted that the number of ships would increase. What they missed is that effective capacity continues shrinking because of longer voyages, port congestion and stronger-than-expected global trade. As a result, charter rates remain near multi-year highs despite fleet growth.

𝗛𝗔𝗥𝗣𝗘𝗫 𝗖𝗼𝗻𝗳𝗶𝗿𝗺𝘀 𝗢𝘂𝗿 𝗧𝗵𝗲𝘀𝗶𝘀

The HARPEX (Harper Petersen Charter Rates Index) tracks worldwide container ship charter rates and is one of the best real-time indicators of supply and demand in the container shipping market.

While many analysts continue predicting oversupply, the HARPEX has continued making new multi-year highs. (See chart below.)

If the market truly had excess vessel capacity, charter rates would be falling, not rising.

Instead, charter rates continue moving higher because effective capacity remains constrained, exactly as our thesis suggests.

Starting in September 2023, $ZIM ordered 48 newbuild container vessels, approximately 80% of which are LNG-powered, with deliveries taking place from June 2026 through December 2028.

At the time, we were not fans of these agreements, believing the company was committing too much capital instead of returning more cash to shareholders. However, the sharp rise in the Harpex Index has completely changed the economics of the deals.

Based on our estimates, the total value of these lease commitments is approximately $4.5 billion. Using current charter market rates, we estimate those contracts are now worth roughly $1.5 billion more than when they were signed.

Based on current short-term charter rates, we estimate these vessels could command roughly double ZIM’s contracted rates if leased today. Even under long-term charter agreements, we estimate they would earn a 30% to 40% premium over the contracted rates.

𝗪𝗵𝘆 $HLAG Wants $ZIM

$HLAG agreed to acquire $ZIM for approximately $4.2 billion.

Adding payments to FIMI, approximately $1 billion of vessel-related commitments, employee retention packages estimated at approximately $500 million, and other integration costs, we estimate $HLAG’s total economic commitment approached $5.5-6.0 billion.

The strategic rationale is obvious.

• One of the youngest LNG fleets in the industry.

• Strong Trans-Pacific franchise.

• Immediate operating synergies.

• Significant earnings contribution.

According to $HLAG’s merger presentation(see below), approximately 35% of combined EBIT would come from $ZIM.

If $HLAG has a market capitalization of approximately $26 billion, and $ZIM contributes approximately 38% of combined EBIT, we believe $ZIM could justify a valuation exceeding $10 billion under comparable valuation multiples.

In addition, $HLAG’s own new LNG vessels are not expected to arrive in meaningful numbers until approximately 2029-2033, making $ZIM’s modern fleet strategically valuable today.

In our view, $HLAG was willing to go through all the buyout headaches because it understood that $ZIM’s value is at least double the approximately $6 billion it would have paid.

𝗘𝗮𝗿𝗻𝗶𝗻𝗴𝘀 𝗘𝘅𝗽𝗲𝗰𝘁𝗮𝘁𝗶𝗼𝗻𝘀

Based on current freight rates, we estimate:

• Q2: $1-2 EPS

• Q3: $6-10 EPS

• Q4: $0-4 EPS

These estimates naturally depend on freight rates remaining near current levels.

𝗗𝗶𝘃𝗶𝗱𝗲𝗻𝗱 𝗘𝘅𝗽𝗲𝗰𝘁𝗮𝘁𝗶𝗼𝗻𝘀

Assuming today’s freight environment continues, we currently expect:

• August 2026: ~$0.30/share
• November 2026: ~$2-3/share
• March 2027: ~$2-3/share

Looking further ahead, we believe $ZIM could also distribute a special dividend of approximately $10-14/share during 2027 or 2028, assuming freight markets remain supportive and management continues executing its capital allocation strategy.

Based on our estimates, that would likely be the highest dividend yield available anywhere in the public equity markets during that period.

We do not expect $ZIM’s share price to skyrocket from current levels. Instead, we believe a modest 15% share price appreciation, combined with dividends, could generate a total return of approximately 100% over the next 24 months.

The majority of shareholder returns, in our view, will come through cash distributions rather than capital appreciation.

Based on our conservative estimates, shareholders could receive approximately 70% of today’s share price back through ordinary and special dividends over the next 24 months, assuming freight markets remain supportive.

Based on our estimates, $ZIM should generate approximately $10 per share in average annual earnings across the shipping cycle, with exceptionally strong years potentially earning more than $22 per share, while weaker years could result in losses of around $2 per share.

The key point is that container shipping is a highly cyclical business. Rather than focusing on any single year, we believe investors should evaluate $ZIM based on its normalized earnings power over a full cycle.

If $HLAG has a market capitalization of approximately $26 billion, and $ZIM contributes approximately 38% of combined EBIT, we believe $ZIM could justify a valuation exceeding $10 billion under comparable valuation multiples.

In addition, $HLAG’s own new LNG vessels are not expected to arrive in meaningful numbers until approximately 2029-2033, making $ZIM’s modern fleet strategically valuable today.

In our view, $HLAG was willing to go through all the buyout headaches because it understood that $ZIM’s value is at least double the approximately $6 billion it would have paid.

𝗛𝗶𝗱𝗱𝗲𝗻 𝗔𝘀𝘀𝗲𝘁𝘀

$ZIM controls approximately 1.08 million containers, of which we estimate approximately 55% are owned and 45% are leased.

The balance sheet carries these assets at approximately $800 million.

Standard containers cost approximately $4,000, while refrigerated containers typically cost approximately $15,000, with specialized units reaching more than $40,000.

Using an estimated average replacement cost of approximately $4,500 per container, the gross replacement value approaches approximately $5 billion.

After adjusting for leased containers, we estimate the economic value of $ZIM’s owned container fleet alone is approximately $3.5-4.0 billion.

𝗖𝗮𝗿 𝗖𝗮𝗿𝗿𝗶𝗲𝗿 𝗕𝘂𝘀𝗶𝗻𝗲𝘀𝘀

$ZIM’s vehicle carrier business is currently operating near peak pricing, generating an estimated $70-80 million per quarter, providing another earnings stream that receives very little attention.

𝗘𝗮𝗿𝗻𝗶𝗻𝗴𝘀 𝗘𝘅𝗽𝗲𝗰𝘁𝗮𝘁𝗶𝗼𝗻𝘀

Based on current freight rates, we estimate:

• Q2: $1-2 EPS

• Q3: $6-10 EPS

• Q4: $0-4 EPS

These estimates naturally depend on freight rates remaining near current levels.

𝗗𝗶𝘃𝗶𝗱𝗲𝗻𝗱 𝗘𝘅𝗽𝗲𝗰𝘁𝗮𝘁𝗶𝗼𝗻𝘀

Assuming today’s freight environment continues, we currently expect:

• August 2026: ~$0.30/share
• November 2026: ~$2-3/share
• March 2027: ~$2-3/share

Looking further ahead, we believe $ZIM could also distribute a special dividend of approximately $10-14/share during 2027 or 2028, assuming freight markets remain supportive and management continues executing its capital allocation strategy.

Based on our estimates, that would likely be the highest dividend yield available anywhere in the public equity markets during that period.

We do not expect $ZIM’s share price to skyrocket from current levels. Instead, we believe a modest 15% share price appreciation, combined with dividends, could generate a total return of approximately 100% over the next 24 months.

The majority of shareholder returns, in our view, will come through cash distributions rather than capital appreciation.

Based on our conservative estimates, shareholders could receive approximately 70% of today’s share price back through ordinary and special dividends over the next 24 months, assuming freight markets remain supportive.

Based on our estimates, $ZIM should generate approximately $10 per share in average annual earnings across the shipping cycle, with exceptionally strong years potentially earning more than $22 per share, while weaker years could result in losses of around $2 per share.

The key point is that container shipping is a highly cyclical business. Rather than focusing on any single year, we believe investors should evaluate $ZIM based on its normalized earnings power over a full cycle.

𝗣𝗼𝗿𝘁𝗳𝗼𝗹𝗶𝗼 𝗔𝗹𝗹𝗼𝗰𝗮𝘁𝗶𝗼𝗻 (Updated): 20%

Ethereum is the federal government and instead of charging 30% tax it charges 1% and lets states and counties charge the bulk of the tax 

Security is the most mispriced asset in blockchain land 

federal states can make it hard for citizens to leave and few (ie US) can enforce worldwide tax - as a US citizen you pay the tax because they can use violence against you

blockchains can’t and never will 

they are by design open source and easy to leave, so they will always struggle to grow GDP via taxation

I don’t see an easy solution to this problem other than being an integrated chain that owns the user relationship and can monetize the flow and enforce some control of who enters and leaves 

Robinhood can do this
Stripe can do this 
Infra crypto-native providers can’t 

And if that’s the case then what’s the point of blockchains if you have a single entity that controls it. Databases all the way down. 

Robinhood is simply replacing citadel and monetizing the flow themselves via robinhood chain - as they should

In order to be successful, you have to do five things. There’s a sequence to it.

1. Have (audacious) goals.
2. Identify and don't tolerate problems.
3. Diagnose the problems to get at their root causes.
4. Design a path to fix those things.
5. Push through to results.

This is what I call looping. Go for your goals, identify your problems, get to the root cause, design a path, and push through. Life is basically just doing that over and over again. If you do that, you'll make the advances.

#Principles #RayDalio  #PersonalGrowth

Brian has been an unwavering north star in Bitcoin and crypto.

His innovation and creativity know no bounds.

The lesson for founders: If the first VC says no... try asking one of their sons or daughters... some call mine the new VC Scions.

tweets.text:
google $GOOG  is struggling to sell it's TPUs, 75% of chips neoclouds use are nvidias but theres a strong case for google winning over the next year: cost.

meta, uber, microsoft and many others have cut back on aggressive AI spending over the last few months, opting for cheaper models - this has saved them $10-100Ms

but the largest cost in using ai models comes from the underlying infrastructure used to serve it to customers: tpus are the cheaper (and more effective) alternative.

my guess is as inference becomes 50%+ of ai token usage, data centers dedicated to inference will purchase a shitload of tpus, growing googles chip marketshare to 10%+ of nvidia's

thread_tweets.text:
And manage your risk if you don't want the market to manage it for you

Personally when I see these dips I have the conviction that they are great entries on a multi-week timeframe and that we're likely to bounce higher in the near future, but there's always the risk of seeing further drawdowns

I want to position myself so that I can withstand the drawdowns and even add if it gets even more extreme

The lines on the chart are roughly my yesterday's entries, being able to add some long exposure on the Korean session dip and then benefit from the bounce is what you want vs getting stopped at the bottom.
An alternative way of doing things is just to play everything super tight and to invalidate longs very quickly instead of taking the drawdowns.

Several different strategies can work, you just have to know in advance what you're playing.
